lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms, if any, providing repeat outsourced operation and management of barley, oat, rye, sorghum, wild-rice, or other 111199 grain production for external landowners or program customers while materially operating the farm.
lens.excluded_activities: Owner-operated farms selling their own crops, passive land or financing vehicles, manage-only agricultural services classified in NAICS 115, wheat, corn, cultivated rice, sweet corn, oilseed-grain combination farms, and one-off custom fieldwork without recurring operating responsibility.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The qualifying model would use recurring farm-management, crop-share, acreage, or production agreements with accountability for a full growing program. Public sources describe commodity production but do not establish a defensible population or contract model for outsourced operators inside 111199.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to repeat outsourced farm operation because the NAICS is primarily grain production for commodity sale, while the fixed lens requires an outsourced service. The crop subsector also distinguishes operate-and-manage farms from manage-only support establishments, making the eligible boundary uncertain.

a.bridge: USDA's all-farm occupation shares and grain-relevant precision-technology adoption are combined with ILO broad-occupation GenAI exposure to infer wage-weighted current opportunity for 111199. This may overstate exposure because field and equipment work dominates and understate it because owner-operator management, agronomy, marketing, and compliance labor is not fully captured in hired-worker data.
a.rationale: AI can assist crop planning, weather and agronomic interpretation, input purchasing, marketing, records, compliance, scheduling, and equipment support. Planting, spraying, irrigation, harvesting, maintenance, and field inspection remain physical, and automated guidance is already widespread on sorghum and other row-crop acreage.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study for 111199 farms, including owner labor, hired labor, current autosteer and variable-rate use, and time spent on agronomy, marketing, records, and machinery, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: USDA labor shares cover all agriculture rather than other-grain farms.
Precision-adoption data include larger field crops and measure existing digitalization, not generative-AI exposure.
The frozen labor-share input is missing, so this primitive cannot be combined with a measured industry labor intensity.

rho.rationale: Planning, recordkeeping, agronomic decision support, procurement, marketing, and maintenance knowledge can be implemented within five years, but rural connectivity, data interoperability, seasonal workflows, farm scale, trust, weather uncertainty, and thin management capacity limit adoption.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-season farm pilots reporting adoption, labor hours, input savings, yield effects, forecast error, equipment integration costs, and operator override rates would materially change the range.
caveats: No five-year implementation study specific to 111199 was found.
USDA shows precision-technology adoption rises sharply with farm size, so LMM applicability may differ from the farm average.

e.rationale: The frozen LMM firm count and labor-share inputs are explicitly missing, and public data do not distinguish commodity-producing farms from firms providing a recurring outsourced operating service. Neither denominator nor eligible numerator can be bounded without replacing the missing dataset input.
e.change_evidence: A verified farm-to-firm roster with normalized EBITDA, land ownership, operating agreements, revenue source, customer recurrence, control eligibility, and NAICS classification is required.
caveats: Farm counts are not equivalent to LMM firms and may include sole proprietors, family operations, and multiple establishments.
Manage-only firms may be classified in agricultural support activities rather than 111199.

s5.rationale: No defensible eligible LMM operating-firm population was identified, so a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability cannot be estimated without confusing land transfers, farm succession, and transfers of a recurring service business.
s5.change_evidence: After establishing eligible firms, principal-operator ages, land and operating-company ownership, family succession plans, lease terms, broker mandates, and five-year control transactions would be required.
caveats: Farm or land sales do not necessarily transfer a recurring outsourced operating business.
Owner age alone would not establish transferability of customer contracts and operations.

q.rationale: No verified in-code outsourced-service population or representative operating agreement was found. Commodity crop prices and farm margins do not reveal contractual sharing of an implemented labor benefit.
q.change_evidence: Verified farm-operation agreements showing fixed fees, crop shares, cost reimbursement, input pass-through, renewal cadence, performance bonuses, and customer concentration are required.
caveats: Commodity-price exposure is distinct from commercial retention of productivity gains.
Terms from custom fieldwork or manage-only firms outside the lens cannot be assumed.

d5.bridge: USDA outlooks for barley, oats, sorghum, millet, and other coarse grains are translated into year-five constant-quality demand for the hypothetical outsourced operating basket. This can overstate the lens if acreage stays owner-operated and understate it if consolidation increases third-party operating mandates.
d5.rationale: Feed, brewing, food, seed, and export uses support recurring grain demand, but individual crops compete with corn, wheat, oilseeds, imports, and alternative acreage. USDA's 2026 outlook raised global barley and oats but lowered sorghum in one marketing year, supporting a near-flat base with meaningful crop and weather dispersion.
d5.change_evidence: USDA Baseline acreage and use projections by included crop, land-rent and operating-contract trends, customer procurement forecasts, and constant-quality service acreage would materially change the range.
caveats: Commodity production outlook is not a direct measure of outsourced farm-operation demand.
Global outlook changes may not translate to U.S. acreage.
Weather and annual crop switching can dominate short-period trends.

o.rationale: Grain production still requires an accountable operator for land access, agronomy, equipment, safety, stewardship, harvest, and delivery. AI and autonomous equipment can reduce labor and decision time, while landowners could self-operate or hire task-specific services instead of a full-service operator.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on autonomous field operations, landowner insourcing, full-farm outsourcing, custom-operator substitution, farm consolidation, and changes in operator responsibility would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The estimate separates generative AI from mechanization and autonomous equipment.
The accountable outsourced operator population itself remains unverified.

narrative.executive_view: 111199 has durable physical grain demand and meaningful digital decision-support potential, but it is fundamentally commodity crop production. Public data do not establish a lower-middle-market population providing repeat outsourced farm operation, and both frozen dataset inputs are missing.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve planning, agronomy, procurement, marketing, compliance, records, and maintenance support. Field operations remain physical and substantial mechanization, including autosteer, is already embedded, limiting the unautomated opportunity.
narrative.value_capture: Commercial retention cannot be bounded without actual farm-operation agreements. Commodity margins, crop prices, and precision-agriculture savings do not specify how a landowner and operator share productivity benefits.
narrative.firm_availability: No defensible LMM denominator or eligible outsourced-operator numerator is available. Farm counts, acreage, and farm succession cannot substitute for a verified transferable operating-company population.
narrative.demand_durability: Barley, oats, sorghum, rye, wild rice, and seed uses provide recurring demand, but crop substitution, weather, exports, and feed economics create variability. Commodity demand does not prove outsourced-service demand.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The key risks are absence of a scalable service target pool, missing labor and firm-count inputs, commodity and weather volatility, land and operator entanglement, thin margins, existing mechanization, classification leakage to agricultural support services, and nontransferable family operations.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring physical demand for feed, brewing, food, seed, and export grains supported by scalable mechanized field operations.
narrative.principal_weakness: No defensible outsourced-service firm population, contract economics, or LMM denominator can be established inside 111199.

sources.title: NAICS Sector 11 archive: 111199 All Other Grain Farming
sources.supports: Industry boundary and included crops such as barley, oats, rye, sorghum, and wild rice, with exclusions for wheat, corn, cultivated rice, and combination farms.

sources.title: Precision Agriculture in the Digital Era: Recent Adoption on U.S. Farms
sources.supports: Adoption of guidance, mapping, and variable-rate technologies, including sorghum acreage, and evidence on labor-saving and farm-size effects.

sources.title: Farm Labor
sources.supports: USDA occupation shares and wages for hired agricultural workers, supervisors, managers, equipment operators, and crop workers.

sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: A global analysis of potential effects on job quantity and quality
sources.supports: Broad occupation-level GenAI exposure and the distinction between technical exposure, augmentation, and realized automation.

sources.title: Corn and Other Feed Grains: Market Outlook
sources.supports: Current outlook direction for barley, oats, millet, sorghum, and the broader coarse-grain complex.

sources.title: Agricultural Workers: Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Physical work requirements, mechanization trends, and projected changes in agricultural equipment-operator and farmworker employment.
